topic_description: Project README.md files — the first document a visitor reads — covering what the project is and why, prerequisites, how to install and run it, common workflows, and where to go next.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

Good READMEs reduce time-to-understanding and time-to-first-success. They are the project’s front door: a README that is readable, correct, safe, and maintained improves adoption, reduces support burden, and prevents costly mistakes.

Principles and trade-offs:
- Readability: Readers scan before they read. Clear structure, scannable headings, and copy-pastable commands matter more than prose elegance. Trade-off: adding a Table of Contents can help long READMEs but adds maintenance overhead. Short, purposeful sections beat a wall of text.
- Maintainability: The README should be stable, delegating deep or fast-moving details to versioned docs. Trade-off: duplicating instructions increases accessibility but risks drift; linking out reduces drift but adds clicks.
- Correctness: Links should resolve, commands should run, versions and supported platforms should be explicit. Trade-off: exhaustively documenting every edge case in the README creates bloat—reserve detail for docs and keep “golden paths” here.
- Safety: Destructive or privilege-elevated commands in READMEs can cause irrecoverable damage. Don’t pipe curl to shell; avoid sudo; never include secrets. Trade-off: showing “quick and dirty” commands is tempting but dangerous; be conservative and explicit.
- Performance (of onboarding): Optimize for speed to first success. A minimal Quickstart (ideally ≤3 commands) is better than a long “theory of operation.” Trade-off: Quickstart may hide configuration complexity; balance with a Configuration section and links to docs.
- Common failure modes:
  - Missing or misleading prerequisites (wrong runtime or tooling versions).
  - Non-copy-pastable commands (shell prompts like “$ ” or line-wraps).
  - Broken links and missing assets.
  - Overuse of badges and images, hindering scanability.
  - README drift (inconsistent with code, LICENSE, or CONTRIBUTING).
  - Unsafe examples (curl | bash, rm -rf, sudo) with no warnings or alternatives.
- Contested areas where reasonable practitioners disagree:
  - Table of Contents for short READMEs: some prefer none to reduce clutter.
  - Line length limits in Markdown: some prioritize plain-text diffability; others don’t.
  - “sudo” in install commands: some allow it for system package managers; others forbid it in examples.
  - Number and placement of badges: branding vs. signal-to-noise.
  - Enforcing language tags on all code fences: strictness vs. convenience.

## Section 2: Rules File

Preamble (scope and audience)
- This rules file defines what “good” looks like for project README.md files intended for public or internal consumption by engineers.
- It targets repositories with code users can run, install, or integrate, and it optimizes for first-time success, safety, and long-term maintainability.

Structure
- Start with a single H1 project title as the first non-empty line. A clear entry point anchors the document.
- Follow the title with a one-sentence elevator pitch in plain language. Readers need immediate context and value.
- Include a “Prerequisites” section listing required runtimes, tools, and explicit versions. Version correctness prevents setup failures.
- Include an “Installation” section with step-by-step commands. Clear steps reduce friction.
- Include a “Quickstart” or “Usage” section that gets to a working run. Speed to first success builds confidence.
- Include a “Configuration” section for environment variables and config files. Centralizing knobs prevents guesswork.
- Include a “Troubleshooting” or “FAQ” section for common errors and fixes. Anticipated issues cut support load.
- Include a “Contributing” section or link to CONTRIBUTING. Clear contribution paths invite help.
- Include a “License” section that names the license and links to LICENSE. Legal clarity is non-negotiable.
- If the README exceeds ~200 lines, include a Table of Contents. Long documents need navigation. (contested)

Content
- State supported platforms and version ranges explicitly. Avoids unsupported environments.
- Show a minimal working example or demo command. Proof of life validates the path.
- Use relative links for repo-local assets and docs. Relative links survive forks and mirrors.
- Provide “Next steps” links to deeper docs or examples. Readers need a path forward.

Commands & Code
- Put commands in fenced code blocks tagged with the correct language (bash, sh, zsh, pwsh, cmd). Syntax hints improve readability and tooling.
- Make commands copy-pastable; do not prefix with shell prompts like “$ ” or “>”. Prompt characters break copy-paste. (contested)
- Keep Quickstart to three commands or fewer; link out for advanced setup. Fewer steps increase completion. (contested)
- Use placeholders in angle brackets (<TOKEN>) and define each once. Consistent placeholders prevent misuse.
- Provide uninstall or cleanup instructions alongside install. Reversibility is part of safety.

Style
- Keep lines to 120 characters or fewer. Short lines improve diffs and readability in terminals. (contested)
- Prefer Markdown over raw HTML; use HTML only when Markdown cannot express the structure. Simpler markup is more portable. (contested)
- Provide descriptive alt text for every image and badge. Alt text improves accessibility and context.
- Limit badges to five or fewer and place them under the title. Too many badges harm scanability. (contested)

Safety
- Never recommend piping curl or wget output into a shell (e.g., curl ... | bash). This pattern is unsafe by default.
- Do not include destructive commands (rm -rf, dd, mkfs, DROP DATABASE) in examples. READMEs must not cause data loss.
- Avoid sudo in example commands. Least privilege prevents accidental system changes. (contested)
- Never include real secrets, tokens, or passwords; use placeholders and demonstrate secure configuration. Public docs must not leak credentials.
- Call out irreversible actions with an explicit WARNING and safer alternatives. Explicit warnings reduce accidents.

Correctness & Links
- Ensure all Markdown links resolve (internal anchors, local files, and external URLs). Broken links break trust.
- Ensure all local images and assets exist and render. Missing assets frustrate users.
- Name the license explicitly and link to the repo’s LICENSE file. Consistency avoids ambiguity.

Maintenance
- Keep the README focused; move deep guides to /docs and link to them. Centralizing detail reduces drift.
- Avoid duplicating instructions that are generated or documented elsewhere; link instead. Duplication rots.
- Keep status badges relevant and current (build, test, coverage). Stale signals mislead.

Troubleshooting & Support
- Link to the issue tracker and support channels. Clear support paths reduce friction.
- List the most common error(s) with fixes or links to solutions. Anticipation prevents dead-ends.
- Provide a way to report security issues privately. Responsible disclosure protects users.

Performance
- Optimize Quickstart for the shortest path to a successful run. Fast success drives adoption.
- Prefer dependency installation methods that use caching (e.g., package managers) over ad-hoc scripts. Faster, more reproducible installs.
- Offer a containerized run option when practical. Containers reduce environment drift. (contested)

## Section 3: Deterministic Validation Structure - Rule — "Start with a single H1 project title as the first non-empty line." - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Trim leading blank lines; the first non-empty line must match regex: ^# [^\n]+$ and there must not be another H1 (line starting with "# ") before any other heading level. Violation if missing or multiple H1s. - Failure mode — First line is "My Project" (no leading "#"). - Limitations — Does not validate title content quality. - Rule — "Include a 'Prerequisites' section listing required runtimes, tools, and explicit versions." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and raw section text. - Check — Find a heading whose text matches /(Prerequisites|Requirements)/i. Within its section, require at least one line containing a semantic version-like pattern (e.g., \b(v?\d+\.\d+(\.\d+)?\+?)\b) or named tool + version (e.g., "Node.js 18+"). Violation if section missing or no versions detected. - Failure mode — Section exists but only says "Install dependencies" with no versions. - Limitations — May miss non-numeric version descriptors ("latest LTS"); may false-positive on unrelated numbers. - Rule — "Include an 'Installation' section with step-by-step commands." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Require a heading matching /Install(ation)?/i and at least one fenced code block within that section. - Failure mode — "Installation" heading present but no code block. - Limitations — Cannot verify commands are correct. - Rule — "Include a 'Quickstart' or 'Usage' section that gets to a working run." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Require a heading matching /(Quick[- ]?start|Usage)/i and at least one fenced code block under it. - Failure mode — No Quickstart/Usage section. - Limitations — Cannot ensure “working run.” - Rule — "Include a 'Configuration' section for environment variables and config files." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Require a heading matching /Config(uration)?/i. - Failure mode — No configuration section. - Limitations — Does not assess completeness. - Rule — "Include a 'Troubleshooting' or 'FAQ' section for common errors and fixes." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Require a heading matching /(Troubleshooting|FAQ|Frequently Asked Questions)/i. - Failure mode — Missing such section. - Limitations — Does not check quality of content. - Rule — "Include a 'Contributing' section or link to CONTRIBUTING." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and file system. - Check — Pass if a heading matching /Contributing/i exists OR the README contains a link to a file named CONTRIBUTING (any extension) in repo root. - Failure mode — No contributing info and no link to CONTRIBUTING. - Limitations — Does not validate contribution guidelines content. - Rule — "Include a 'License' section that names the license and links to LICENSE." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and file system. - Check — Require a heading matching /License/i AND a link whose target matches ^(./)?LICENSE(\.[A-Za-z0-9._-]+)?$ present in repo. Violation if missing. - Failure mode — "License" section says "MIT" but no link to LICENSE file. - Limitations — Does not verify license correctness. - Rule — "If the README exceeds ~200 lines, include a Table of Contents. (contested)" - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — If total non-empty lines > 200, require a heading matching /(Table of Contents|Contents)/i. - Failure mode — 350-line README with no ToC. - Limitations — Length is a heuristic; some long READMEs remain navigable. Content - Rule — "State supported platforms and version ranges explicitly." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Search for a section (e.g., Prerequisites or a dedicated Support/Compatibility section) containing patterns like "(Windows|Linux|macOS|ARM|x86_64)" and version-like patterns. Violation if none found anywhere. - Failure mode — README lacks any explicit OS/runtime versions. - Limitations — May false-positive or -negative depending on phrasing. - Rule — "Use relative links for repo-local assets and docs." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and file system. - Check — For links whose host is empty (relative) vs. absolute; flag absolute links pointing to the same repo (e.g., https://github.com/org/repo/blob/...). Violation if README links to its own repo using absolute URLs for files that exist locally. - Failure mode — Linking to docs via full GitHub URL instead of ./docs/guide.md. - Limitations — Sometimes absolute links are intentional (cross-fork or permalink). Commands & Code - Rule — "Put commands in fenced code blocks tagged with the correct language (bash, sh, zsh, pwsh, cmd)." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — For every fenced code block whose content matches command-like patterns (contains lines starting with common commands or includes characters like &&, |, # comments), require a non-empty info string that is one of [bash, sh, zsh, shell, pwsh, powershell, cmd]. Violation if missing or not in set. - Failure mode — ``` followed by commands but no language tag. - Limitations — Detecting “command-like” is heuristic; may miss or overflag. - Rule — "Make commands copy-pastable; do not prefix with shell prompts like “$ ” or “>”. (contested)" - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — In code blocks tagged as shell-like, flag any line starting with /^\s*[$>#]\s+/. - Failure mode — Line like "$ npm install". - Limitations — Some copyable prompts may be intentionally included; Windows “>” also used in redirection. - Rule — "Keep Quickstart to three commands or fewer; link out for advanced setup. (contested)" - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — In the Quickstart section’s first shell-like fenced block, count non-empty, non-comment lines; pass if count ≤ 3. - Failure mode — Quickstart block has 7 commands. - Limitations — Multi-line commands and line continuations complicate counting. - Rule — "Use placeholders in angle brackets () and define each once." - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Extract tokens matching /<([A-Z0-9_:-]+)>/; ensure a “Configuration” or a dedicated “Placeholders” subsection defines each (by name or table). Violation if unmatched tokens exist. - Failure mode — Uses but no definition. - Limitations — May miss free-form definitions. - Rule — "Provide uninstall or cleanup instructions alongside install." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST. - Check — Require a heading matching /(Uninstall|Remove|Cleanup)/i OR an uninstall subsection under Installation. - Failure mode — Install exists, no uninstall. - Limitations — Cannot confirm instructions actually reverse install. Style - Rule — "Keep lines to 120 characters or fewer. (contested)" - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Flag any line with length > 120 characters (excluding URLs within code blocks if whitelisting them). Violation if any. - Failure mode — A paragraph line is 180 chars long. - Limitations — May flag long URLs or tables; consider excluding code blocks and link reference definitions. - Rule — "Prefer Markdown over raw HTML; use HTML only when Markdown cannot express the structure. (contested)" - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Flag presence of raw HTML tags other than a small whitelist (e.g.,

for layout. - Limitations — Some READMEs intentionally use HTML for badges or layout. - Rule — "Provide descriptive alt text for every image and badge." - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — For all image syntaxes ![alt](src), flag if alt is empty or whitespace. - Failure mode — ![](logo.png). - Limitations — Cannot judge description quality. - Rule — "Limit badges to five or fewer and place them under the title. (contested)" - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Within the first 20 non-empty lines after the H1, count images whose src matches common badge hosts (shields.io, badgen.net, github.com/.../badge). Violation if count > 5. - Failure mode — 9 shields.io badges under the title. - Limitations — Non-standard badges may be missed; line window heuristic. Safety - Rule — "Never recommend piping curl or wget output into a shell (e.g., curl ... | bash)." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and raw code blocks. - Check — In any code block or inline code, flag patterns: /(curl|wget)[^|]+(\|\s*(sudo\s+)?(sh|bash|zsh|pwsh|powershell))/i and Powershell equivalents (iex (iwr ...)). - Failure mode — curl -fsSL https://... | bash - Limitations — Legitimate uses in narrative might false-positive; restrict to code blocks. - Rule — "Do not include destructive commands (rm -rf, dd, mkfs, DROP DATABASE) in examples." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and raw code blocks. - Check — Flag lines matching dangerous patterns: /\brm\s+-rf\b|(^|\s)dd\s+if=|(^|\s)mkfs(\.| )|DROP\s+DATABASE/i. - Failure mode — rm -rf ~/.config/app - Limitations — Some safe contexts (containers, temp dirs) still flagged. - Rule — "Avoid sudo in example commands. (contested)" - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and raw code blocks. - Check — In shell-like blocks, flag any line starting with optional whitespace then sudo\b. - Failure mode — sudo make install - Limitations — Some package manager instructions genuinely require sudo. - Rule — "Never include real secrets, tokens, or passwords; use placeholders and demonstrate secure configuration." - Signal — raw README.md and output of a secret scanner. - Check — Run gitleaks or trufflehog against README; any detected secret is a violation. - Failure mode — export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=ABCD... - Limitations — False positives on dummy-looking secrets; configure allowlist. Violation if missing or 4xx/5xx. - Failure mode — ![Diagram](./assets/diagram.png) when file absent. - Limitations — Private or auth-protected assets will fail HEAD. - Rule — "Name the license explicitly and link to the repo’s LICENSE file." - Signal — parsed Markdown AST and file system. - Check — Presence of /License/i heading and a link to LICENSE* in repo root; file must exist. - Failure mode — "License: Apache-2.0" with no link and no LICENSE file. - Limitations — Repos using non-standard locations may be missed. Violation if broken or “unknown”. - Failure mode — Build badge returns 404 or “unknown”. - Limitations — Some providers throttle or require auth; statuses may be temporarily red. Troubleshooting & Support - Rule — "Link to the issue tracker and support channels." - Signal — raw README.md. - Check — Presence of a URL matching GitHub/GitLab issues pattern or a “Support” email/URL. Regex examples: github\.com/.+/.+/issues, gitlab\.com/.+/.+/-/issues. - Failure mode — No issues link anywhere. - Limitations — Self-hosted trackers require custom patterns. - Rule — "List the most common error(s) with fixes or links to solutions." - Signal — Not suitable for deterministic checking. - Check — Not applicable. - Failure mode — N/A. - Limitations — Requires human judgment. (contested)" - Signal — Not reliably checkable across all projects. - Check — Not applicable. - Failure mode — N/A. - Limitations — Project applicability varies. - Rule — "Optimize Quickstart for the shortest path to a successful run." - Signal — Partially checkable via command count (see Commands & Code Quickstart rule); otherwise human judgment. - Check — Use the ≤3 commands check referenced earlier. - Failure mode — 10-step Quickstart. - Limitations — Steps may be necessary; success cannot be proven mechanically.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

README files serve as the front door to a project, providing first impressions to users, contributors, and maintainers. Good README files excel in readability, maintainability, correctness, safety, and performance, while acknowledging common failure modes and points of contention among practitioners.

**Readability** is paramount in a README. This is the first document many users encounter, and clear, concise language improves user engagement and retention. Bulleted or numbered lists improve skimmability, while an organized structure reduces cognitive overload.

**Maintainability** comes from using consistent formatting, clear sections, and straightforward instructions, allowing future contributors to keep the README updated easily. A well-structured README can quickly bring new maintainers up to speed without extensive handover.

**Correctness** is crucial. Outdated or incorrect information can frustrate users or lead to failures in implementation. Regularly revising the README to align with the latest project changes or best practices is a must.

**Safety** is often overlooked but essentially involves ensuring that installation and usage instructions consider dependencies and other environmental requirements to prevent users from running harmful or incompatible commands.

**Performance** typically relates less to execution time and more to how effectively the documentation conveys essential information. Unnecessary verbosity can clutter important steps or instructions.

Common failure modes include:
- Lack of clarity or missing information.
- Overly technical jargon, excluding non-expert users.
- Failing to update after changes to the project.

Contentions exist particularly around:
- The necessity of examples: some argue that examples are critical for understanding, while others think they can bloat the README.
- The degree of detail for installation instructions: while some advocate for exhaustive instructions to guide less experienced users, others support a more concise approach that encourages exploration.
responses.rules_file:
## Section 2: Rules File

This rules file is intended for engineers and AI coding assistants as a reference on best practices for creating and maintaining effective README files.

### Structure
- Do include a project title and brief description. This sets the context for what the project is about.
- Do use headings and subheadings to organize sections. This improves navigability and readability.
- Do provide a table of contents for extensive READMEs. It allows users to find relevant information quickly.
- Do include sections such as Prerequisites, Installation, Usage, and Contact Information. These provide a clear roadmap for users.

### Content
- Do detail prerequisites explicitly. This ensures users can set up their environments correctly without guesswork.
- Do include installation instructions with code snippets. This provides clarity and aids reproducibility.
- Do provide usage examples where applicable. Clear examples facilitate user understanding and adoption (contested).
- Do explain where to go next after setup or usage, such as contributing guidelines or links to documentation.

### Style
- Do use Markdown for formatting. This makes the README visually appealing and more readable.
- Don't use excessive jargon or technical terms without explanations. This ensures inclusivity for all user skill levels.
- Do use a consistent tense and voice throughout the document for professional tone and clarity.

### Safety
- Do mention any potential risks or important warnings related to the installation or usage of the project. This keeps users informed and safe from potential issues.

### Maintenance
- Do regularly review and update the README as the project evolves. This prevents misinformation and keeps the documentation relevant.
- Don't let issues go unaddressed. Promptly correct or clarify any inaccuracies that users highlight.
responses.deterministic_checks:
## Section 3: Deterministic Validation

- **Rule**: Do use headings and subheadings to organize sections.
  - **Signal**: raw source file.
  - **Check**: Scan the text for markdown heading syntax (e.g., `#`, `##`, `###`). A violation occurs if no headings are found in the README.
  - **Failure mode**: README reads like a continuous block of text with no structural organization. Example: "This project is a problem solver it solves A, B and C."
  - **Limitations**: Some valid READMEs may not use markdown headings heavily but still may be well-structured.

- **Rule**: Do provide a table of contents for extensive READMEs. 
  - **Signal**: raw source file.
  - **Check**: Look for a section beginning with "Table of Contents" and assess if it links to other sections. A violation occurs if a README exceeds 500 words without a TOC.
  - **Failure mode**: README does not include a TOC, making it cumbersome to navigate. Example: A long list of instructions that users struggle to sift through.
  - **Limitations**: Some short READMEs may not require a TOC, and this could lead to false positives.

- **Rule**: Do use Markdown for formatting.
  - **Signal**: raw source file.
  - **Check**: Analyze the file specifically for markdown syntax. A violation is when less than 60% of formatting uses markdown elements (e.g., lists, headings).
  - **Failure mode**: README looks plain and uninviting, lacking basic Markdown elements. Example: "This project has a setup, usage."
  - **Limitations**: Projects with custom formatting may not comply but still provide good readability through other means.

- **Rule**: Do mention potential risks or important warnings related to the project.
  - **Signal**: raw source file.
  - **Check**: Check for specific keywords such as "WARNING," "NOTE," or "CAUTION" followed by relevant content. A violation occurs if none are present.
  - **Failure mode**: README lacks any safety notices which could lead to user errors. Example: "This project may require elevated permissions to run."
  - **Limitations**: Could overlook less formal warnings phrased differently, leading to a false negative.

responses.reasoning:
## Section 1: Reasoning

A README is a contract with your reader's attention. It has sixty seconds to answer three questions: What is this? Is it for me? How do I try it? Everything else is secondary.

**Readability.** The dominant failure mode is not brevity but disorganization. Readers scan, they don't read. That means a one-line description at the top, a visible table of contents for anything longer than a screen, and headings that name tasks ("Install", "Run the tests") rather than concepts ("Architecture"). Prose should be in second person, imperative where possible, and written at roughly an eighth-grade level. Long paragraphs are where clarity goes to die — prefer lists and fenced code blocks.

**Correctness.** The single most common defect in READMEs is stale instructions: commands that no longer work, version numbers that have drifted, screenshots of a UI that was redesigned. Every command in a README is a claim the project makes about itself, and every claim rots. The mitigation is to keep commands minimal, copy-pasteable, and ideally exercised by CI (e.g., a smoke-test job that runs the quickstart). Version pins should be expressed as minimums where possible, and the "last tested with" date or CI badge communicates recency.

**Maintainability.** READMEs drift when they duplicate content that lives elsewhere — API docs, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, architecture decision records. The rule of thumb: the README links, it does not mirror. Keep it short enough that contributors will actually update it. A 2000-line README is a documentation smell; split it.

**Safety.** `curl | sh` installers, commands that require `sudo`, and examples that disable TLS verification all belong with loud warnings or not at all. Do not embed secrets, even fake-looking ones, in example config; readers copy-paste. Destructive commands (`rm -rf`, `DROP TABLE`) need explicit warnings.

**Performance.** Performance of the README itself: image weight. A 4 MB hero GIF makes the repo page unusable on mobile. Use optimized assets, lazy-loaded where the renderer supports it, and prefer asciicast or SVG to animated GIFs for terminal demos.

**Contested areas.** Badges — some projects drown in them, others consider them noise; I think three is the ceiling. Emoji in headings — widely used, genuinely aids scanning, but hostile to screen readers and grep; I'd avoid them. Table of contents for short READMEs — GitHub auto-generates one in the sidebar, so a hand-maintained TOC for anything under ~500 lines is pure maintenance cost. The "quickstart vs. comprehensive" tension: I favor quickstart-in-README, comprehensive-in-`/docs`, but monorepos and libraries with no external doc site legitimately break this rule.

**Common failure modes.** (1) Burying the "what is this" under installation instructions. (2) Assuming the reader already has your toolchain. (3) Copy-pasted boilerplate sections ("Contributing", "License") that say nothing specific. (4) Screenshots without alt text. (5) Broken relative links after a directory restructure. (6) A "Features" bullet list used as a substitute for explaining what the project *does*.

## Section 2: Rules File

# README.md Rules

**Scope:** Project-level `README.md` files in source repositories. **Audience:** Engineers writing or reviewing project documentation, and AI assistants generating it.

## Structure

- Put the project name as the only H1, on line 1. Readers and tools both expect the title there.
- Follow the H1 with a single-sentence description of what the project is. This is the most-read line in the document.
- Place a "Quickstart" or "Install" section above any conceptual or architectural content. Readers come to do, not to learn.
- Use heading levels sequentially (H1 → H2 → H3); never skip a level. Accessibility tools and Markdown renderers rely on hierarchy.
- Include a table of contents only when the rendered document exceeds roughly 500 lines. (contested) Below that threshold, GitHub's auto-TOC suffices.
- Put "License" and "Contributing" as links to separate files, not inline sections. Keeps the README focused and the legal text canonical.

## Content

- State the problem the project solves before listing features. Features answer "how"; problems answer "why I should care".
- List prerequisites (language runtime, OS, external services) with minimum versions before any install command. Unmet prerequisites are the top cause of failed first-runs.
- Give a copy-pasteable install command block that works on a clean machine. If setup requires more than five commands, it belongs in a script.
- Show a minimal runnable example that produces visible output. "Hello world" beats a feature tour.
- Document how to run the tests. If a contributor can't verify their change, they won't contribute.
- Link to CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, and architecture docs rather than restating them. Single source of truth.
- State the license by name in the README body (e.g., "MIT") even when linked. Name-level information should not require a click.

## Style

- Write commands as fenced code blocks with a language tag (```bash, ```console, ```sql). Tagged blocks enable syntax highlighting and copy-button behavior.
- Prefix shell examples with `$ ` only when showing output inline; otherwise omit the prompt. Bare commands are easier to copy.
- Use second person ("you run", "you'll see") and imperative mood for instructions. Matches reader expectations.
- Keep lines under 120 characters in the source. Reviewers read diffs, not rendered output.
- Don't use emoji in headings. (contested) They break grep, anchor links, and screen readers.
- Limit badges to three at the top: build status, package version, license. (contested) More is decoration.

## Safety

- Don't include `curl … | sh` installers without also documenting a manual alternative. Pipe-to-shell is a security posture users should opt into.
- Don't put real secrets, tokens, or internal hostnames in examples. Readers copy-paste; reviewers miss it.
- Mark destructive commands (`rm -rf`, `DROP`, `--force`) with an explicit warning callout. Accidental destruction is unrecoverable.
- Don't instruct readers to disable TLS verification, SELinux, or firewalls as a workaround. Document the real fix or file the bug.

## Correctness & Maintenance

- Ensure every command in the README runs successfully on a supported platform as of the last commit. Stale commands are the most common README defect.
- Keep relative links valid after file moves. Broken links are a signal the document isn't maintained.
- Don't hand-maintain a duplicate of `--help` output. Regenerate it or link to it.
- Update the README in the same commit as the behavior change it documents. Separate commits mean the docs lose the race.

## Performance (of the document itself)

- Keep embedded images under 500 KB each, and total README assets under 2 MB. Repo page load matters on mobile and in low-bandwidth regions.
- Prefer SVG or asciicast for terminal demos over animated GIF. Smaller, scalable, and selectable text.
- Provide alt text for every image. Required for accessibility; useful when images fail to load.

## Length

- Keep the README under 500 lines. (contested) Anything longer belongs in `/docs` with the README linking in.
